medications.indication_rationale: First-line empiric oral antibiotic for uncomplicated acute cystitis in a non-pregnant adult woman (per Therapeutic Guidelines). The source note prescribed nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for 5 days.
medications.dose_route_frequency: nitrofurantoin (modified-release) 100 mg orally, twice daily, for 5 days
contraindications_in_this_case: None identified in the reported history. Nitrofurantoin should be avoided with significant renal impairment (reduced efficacy and higher toxicity) and at term in pregnancy — neither reported here; renal function and pregnancy status should be checked before prescribing.
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Urinary alkalinisers (the OTC sachet the patient took, e.g. sodium citrate/citric acid) alkalinise the urine and REDUCE nitrofurantoin's antibacterial activity — they should not be co-recommended with nitrofurantoin.

medications.indication_rationale: Acceptable first-line alternative for uncomplicated cystitis where nitrofurantoin is unsuitable; unlike nitrofurantoin its activity is not reduced by urinary alkalinisers. Avoid in the first trimester of pregnancy.
medications.dose_route_frequency: trimethoprim 300 mg orally, once daily, for 3 days
contraindications_in_this_case: None reported; avoid in early pregnancy (folate antagonism) — pregnancy status should be confirmed.

medications.drug_name: urinary alkaliniser (sodium citrate / citric acid, e.g. Ural)
medications.indication_rationale: Although often used for symptomatic relief of dysuria, an alkaliniser should NOT be co-recommended with nitrofurantoin (the chosen antibiotic) because it reduces nitrofurantoin efficacy. Evidence for symptomatic alkalinisers is weak at best. If symptomatic relief is wanted, use simple analgesia and hydration, or pair the alkaliniser with a non-nitrofurantoin agent.
contraindications_in_this_case: Reduces efficacy of the co-prescribed nitrofurantoin; caution in cardiac/renal impairment due to sodium load.

medications.indication_rationale: Simple analgesia for the pain of dysuria and suprapubic discomfort while the antibiotic takes effect. No interaction with the antibiotic.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol 1 g orally, up to four times daily as needed (maximum 4 g in 24 hours)

medications.indication_rationale: A fluoroquinolone is NOT appropriate first-line therapy for uncomplicated cystitis — it is reserved for resistant or complicated infections. Using it here is antimicrobial overuse with disproportionate adverse-effect risk (tendinopathy, QT, C. difficile).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Increase fluid intake (water) to help flush the bladder while treated.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Adequate hydration supports symptom relief and urinary flushing. Note: the source note's 'exactly 250 mL every hour for 12 hours' is over-prescriptive and not evidence-based; general good hydration is sufficient.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wipe from front to back after using the toilet.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces transfer of faecal flora (E. coli) to the urethra, lowering re-infection risk.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Complete the entire antibiotic course even if symptoms resolve within a day or two.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prevents incomplete eradication, relapse and resistance.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Void after sexual intercourse and avoid holding urine for long periods.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Simple measures that reduce recurrence of lower UTIs.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a bladder infection. Because it's a telehealth chat, the diagnosis is presumptive (based on your symptoms) — a urine test/culture would confirm it, and results take 48–72 hours if one is sent.
The burning should begin to ease within about 24 hours of starting antibiotics, and the frequency should settle over 2–3 days as the bladder irritation calms down.
Finish the whole course of antibiotics even if you feel completely better after a couple of days.

Go to an emergency department or call for urgent help if you develop severe back or flank pain, high fever with shaking chills, or vomiting so you can't keep the medication down — these can mean the infection has moved to the kidney.
See your GP if blood in the urine does not clear after the first few doses, or if symptoms aren't improving after 48 hours.

follow_up_plan.interval: 3–5 days if symptoms have not completely resolved; sooner if worsening
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Symptom resolution, response to antibiotic, any red-flag features; send urine culture and review antibiotic choice if not improving.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Development of fever/rigors, flank/back pain, vomiting, inability to keep medication down, persistent visible blood in urine, or no improvement after 48 hours of antibiotics.

must_include_items: Recognise the presentation as a likely lower urinary tract infection (bladder infection)
Screen for pyelonephritis red flags (fever/rigors, flank pain, vomiting) before treating as uncomplicated
Arrange access to a first-line oral antibiotic (nitrofurantoin OR trimethoprim) via telehealth prescription, GP, or pharmacist UTI pathway
Advise completing the full antibiotic course
Give a clear pyelonephritis/urosepsis safety-net with specific escalation triggers
Provide symptomatic and hygiene/prevention advice (hydration, analgesia, wipe front-to-back)
acceptable_alternatives: Either nitrofurantoin (100 mg BD 5 days) or trimethoprim (300 mg daily 3 days) as the empiric agent
Deferring antibiotics pending a pharmacist/GP UTI assessment is acceptable provided timely access and safety-netting are arranged
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for pyelonephritis/systemic features
Failing to safety-net for kidney infection / urosepsis
Failing to advise completing the antibiotic course
Not confirming the patient is non-pregnant / not otherwise 'complicated' before applying uncomplicated management
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging an uncomplicated cystitis to an emergency department or calling 000
Prescribing a fluoroquinolone (e.g. ciprofloxacin) or broad-spectrum agent first-line
Co-recommending a urinary alkaliniser together with nitrofurantoin (reduces its efficacy)
Ordering imaging or blood tests for a first uncomplicated lower UTI
Inventing a specific dose for a paediatric patient (not applicable here — adult)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising a lower UTI, screening for pyelonephritis, arranging first-line antibiotic access, and safety-netting — WITHOUT over-triaging to ED/000. This is a low-acuity, telehealth/GP-manageable case: correct self-care-plus-prescription management is the passing answer, and escalation to emergency care is an over-triage error.
